questions.stem: A 61-year-old woman presents 3 days after parathyroid adenoma resection with perioral numbness, hand cramping, and palpitations. Exam: carpopedal spasm after cuff inflation, facial twitch on tapping. ECG: prolonged QT. Calcium 1.72 mmol/L, albumin 39, phosphate 1.6, Mg 0.70. Best initial management?
options: Oral vitamin D3 and outpatient follow-up
IV calcium gluconate with cardiac monitoring
questions.explain: Acute symptomatic hypocalcemia (tetany, long QT) from hungry bone syndrome after parathyroidectomy → IV calcium gluconate with monitoring (and correct magnesium). A bisphosphonate would worsen hypocalcemia.

questions.stem: A 77-year-old man has fatigue, constipation, polyuria, and mild confusion. Calcium 3.05 mmol/L, albumin 40, phosphate low-normal, PTH 13 pmol/L (high), 25-OH vitamin D normal. Best next step in acute management?
questions.explain: PTH-dependent hypercalcemia (primary hyperparathyroidism). Acute symptomatic hypercalcemia is treated first with IV isotonic saline for volume + calciuresis. Surgery may be definitive later; it isn't the first acute step.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman has a 2.0 cm thyroid nodule with palpitations, heat intolerance, and tremor. TSH is suppressed. Ultrasound shows a solid nodule without microcalcifications or abnormal nodes. Next best investigation?
questions.explain: With a suppressed TSH, get a radionuclide scan first. A hot (functioning) nodule is rarely malignant, so FNA is not the immediate step — treat the thyrotoxicosis instead.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman has a 1.2 cm thyroid nodule; TSH normal. Ultrasound: hypoechoic solid nodule with irregular margins and microcalcifications, no suspicious nodes. Next best step?
questions.explain: Normal TSH + a highly suspicious nodule (hypoechoic, irregular margins, microcalcifications) at ≥1 cm → FNA. A radionuclide scan is for suppressed TSH; only cytology rules out cancer.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old postmenopausal woman (BMI 23, no fractures, minimal alcohol, non-smoker) asks about BMD testing. Her mother had a low-trauma hip fracture at 74. Which statement is most appropriate?
options: No BMD until age 70 without a personal fracture
BMD is indicated: age 50–64 with a parental hip fracture is a clinical risk factor
questions.explain: A parental hip fracture is a key clinical risk factor; with age 50–64 it supports BMD testing. Starting a drug without BMD or fracture-risk assessment would be premature.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old woman has femoral-neck T-score −1.4 and lumbar −1.8. She develops back pain; spine x-ray shows a T12 compression fracture after bending to lift a laundry basket. Basic labs normal. Best interpretation and added test?
options: Clinically significant osteoporosis; consider SPEP for myeloma
Normal age-related loss; repeat DXA in 10 years
questions.explain: A low-trauma vertebral fracture is osteoporosis regardless of an osteopenic T-score, and warrants treatment. Vertebral fractures can be the presentation of multiple myeloma → consider SPEP.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with newly diagnosed osteoporosis, normal renal function, no esophageal disease, and no dental procedures planned needs first-line therapy. Best option and counseling?
options: Alendronate weekly; take with water on an empty stomach and stay upright 30–60 min
Denosumab; stop whenever he feels better
Teriparatide; avoid calcium and vitamin D
Calcitonin nasal spray; first-line for hip-fracture prevention
questions.explain: An oral bisphosphonate (alendronate) is first-line. Counsel: morning, empty stomach, full glass of water, remain upright 30–60 min to prevent esophagitis and aid absorption.

questions.stem: A 79-year-old woman on denosumab q6 months is 4 months overdue for her injection and wants to stop because she feels well. What is the most important concern?
options: No concern; denosumab stays in bone for decades
Rebound bone loss and vertebral fractures after stopping/delaying
It causes permanent hypoparathyroidism if continued
questions.explain: Unlike bisphosphonates, denosumab is not retained in bone; stopping or delaying causes a rebound rise in turnover with rapid bone loss and vertebral fractures. Re-dose promptly or transition to a bisphosphonate.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man has deep aching pain in a bowed, warm, mildly erythematous left tibia. X-ray: mixed lytic/sclerotic lesions with cortical thickening. ALP elevated; calcium and phosphate normal. Best diagnosis and treatment?
options: Paget disease; one dose of IV zoledronic acid
questions.explain: Bowing, warmth, bone pain, mixed lytic/sclerotic cortical thickening, and an isolated high ALP with normal calcium/phosphate are classic for Paget disease. First-line is a single IV zoledronic acid dose.

questions.stem: A 36-year-old woman had a total thyroidectomy and radioactive iodine for papillary thyroid carcinoma. Which surveillance best assesses biochemical and structural recurrence?
options: Thyroglobulin with anti-thyroglobulin antibodies plus neck ultrasound
questions.explain: Differentiated thyroid cancer follow-up uses thyroglobulin (+ anti-thyroglobulin antibodies) for biochemical recurrence and neck ultrasound for structural recurrence. Calcitonin is the marker for medullary cancer.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman has calcium 2.70 mmol/L found incidentally, PTH high-normal, and a 24-hour urine calcium that is LOW. Her relatives have mild hypercalcemia. Which is most likely?
questions.explain: Mild PTH-dependent hypercalcemia with a low urine calcium and a family history points to FHH (a calcium-sensing receptor defect). Distinguishing it from primary HPT with the urine calcium / Ca-creatinine clearance ratio avoids unnecessary parathyroidectomy.

questions.stem: A hospitalized patient with albumin 24 g/L (low) has a total calcium of 2.05 mmol/L and no symptoms. What is the most appropriate interpretation?
options: Likely normal once corrected for albumin (or check ionized calcium)
questions.explain: Low albumin falsely lowers total calcium. Correct for albumin (~+0.2 mmol/L per 10 g/L below normal) or measure ionized calcium; the corrected value here is near-normal, and the patient is asymptomatic.

questions.stem: A patient with chronic alcohol use has symptomatic hypocalcemia that fails to correct despite repeated IV calcium. Which electrolyte must be checked and replaced?
questions.explain: Hypomagnesemia impairs PTH secretion/action and causes refractory hypocalcemia — calcium won't correct until magnesium is replaced. Common in alcohol use and malnutrition.

questions.stem: Denosumab reduces bone resorption by which mechanism?
options: Binding mineralized bone and triggering osteoclast apoptosis
questions.explain: Denosumab is an anti-RANKL monoclonal antibody — it acts like OPG, blocking osteoclast formation/activity. Bisphosphonates bind bone and cause osteoclast apoptosis; teriparatide stimulates osteoblasts; romosozumab inhibits sclerostin.

questions.stem: Which osteoporosis drug is an anabolic agent that stimulates bone formation and is avoided in Paget disease and prior skeletal radiation?
questions.explain: Teriparatide (intermittent PTH analog) is anabolic. It is avoided in hypercalcemia, Paget disease, prior skeletal radiation, and skeletal malignancy (osteosarcoma concern).

questions.stem: A thyroid FNA returns medullary carcinoma. Beyond surgery, which additional evaluation is essential?
options: Screen for pheochromocytoma and RET mutation (MEN2)
questions.explain: Medullary thyroid cancer (C cells, calcitonin) is associated with MEN2 (RET) — screen for a pheochromocytoma (operate on it first) and test RET. Thyroglobulin and radioactive iodine apply to differentiated (follicular-cell) cancers, not medullary.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old woman starting long-term high-dose prednisone for vasculitis has no prior fracture. What is the most appropriate bone-health step?
options: Assess fracture risk and start calcium/vitamin D ± a bisphosphonate
questions.explain: Chronic glucocorticoids markedly raise fracture risk (impaired formation + increased resorption). Optimize calcium/vitamin D and assess risk; many high-risk patients warrant a bisphosphonate as glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis prophylaxis.

questions.stem: A very-high-risk osteoporosis patient is considered for romosozumab. Which history most strongly cautions against it?
questions.explain: Romosozumab (sclerostin inhibitor) carries a cardiovascular risk signal — avoid with a recent MI or stroke. GERD favours avoiding oral bisphosphonates (not romosozumab), and vitamin D deficiency is corrected, not a contraindication.

questions.stem: Which medication is a recognised PTH-dependent cause of mild hypercalcemia?
questions.explain: Thiazides reduce urinary calcium excretion and can unmask/cause mild hypercalcemia (a PTH-dependent-pattern cause, along with lithium). Loop diuretics do the opposite (calciuretic).

questions.stem: Which biochemical pattern best distinguishes Paget disease from osteomalacia?
options: Paget: normal calcium/phosphate with high ALP; osteomalacia: low/low-normal calcium and phosphate with high ALP
questions.explain: Paget has an isolated high ALP with normal calcium and phosphate. Osteomalacia (defective mineralization, often vitamin D deficiency) tends to show low/low-normal calcium and phosphate with a high ALP and characteristic Looser zones.

cards.prompt: Contrast cortical and trabecular bone and their clinical fracture relevance.
cards.answer: Cortical (compact, ~80% of mass) — long-bone shafts, hip, radius; preferentially lost in hyperparathyroidism. Trabecular (spongy, fast-remodeling) — vertebrae and hip; the classic osteoporotic fracture sites.

cards.prompt: What do RANKL and OPG do, and which drug mimics OPG?
cards.answer: RANKL drives osteoclast formation/activity; OPG is a decoy receptor that brakes it. Denosumab is an anti-RANKL antibody — a pharmacologic OPG.

cards.prompt: Summarize PTH's net effects on calcium and phosphate.
cards.answer: Raises serum calcium and lowers phosphate: ↑bone resorption, ↑renal Ca reabsorption, ↑phosphate excretion, and ↑activation of vitamin D.

cards.prompt: Why does a low-trauma hip or vertebral fracture diagnose osteoporosis?
cards.answer: It indicates bone fragility and high fracture risk regardless of the T-score, so it is clinically osteoporosis even when BMD is only osteopenic — and warrants treatment.

cards.prompt: What is the difference between a T-score and a Z-score?
cards.answer: T-score compares to a young-adult reference (used in postmenopausal women / men ≥50). Z-score compares to age/sex-matched peers; a low Z-score prompts a search for secondary causes.

cards.prompt: What baseline labs screen for secondary osteoporosis, and when add SPEP?
cards.answer: Calcium+albumin, creatinine/eGFR, ALP, CBC, TSH, 25-OH vitamin D; add SPEP especially with a vertebral fracture to evaluate for multiple myeloma.

cards.prompt: How should oral alendronate be taken, and why?
cards.answer: Morning, empty stomach, full glass of water, remain upright 30–60 min, avoid other food/drugs in that window — to prevent esophagitis and improve absorption.

cards.prompt: Why can't denosumab simply be stopped?
cards.answer: It isn't retained in bone like bisphosphonates; stopping or delaying causes rebound bone turnover with rapid loss and vertebral fractures — transition to a bisphosphonate instead.

cards.prompt: Why correct calcium for albumin?
cards.answer: Only ionized calcium is active; low albumin lowers total calcium without lowering ionized calcium. Correct total calcium for albumin or measure ionized calcium directly.

cards.prompt: How does PTH split the hypercalcemia differential?
cards.answer: High/inappropriately normal PTH = PTH-dependent (primary/tertiary HPT, FHH, lithium, thiazides). Suppressed PTH = PTH-independent (malignancy via PTHrP/osteolysis, vitamin D excess, granulomatous disease).

cards.prompt: Outline acute hypercalcemia management.
cards.answer: IV isotonic saline first (volume + calciuresis); calcitonin for rapid short-term bridging; IV bisphosphonate for durable control; glucocorticoids for granulomatous/calcitriol causes; dialysis if severe/refractory; treat the cause.

cards.prompt: What is hungry bone syndrome?
cards.answer: Profound hypocalcemia after parathyroidectomy for severe hyperparathyroidism — removing chronic PTH excess lets high-turnover bone rapidly take up calcium and phosphate.

cards.prompt: Name the classic signs of hypocalcemia.
cards.answer: Perioral numbness, paresthesias, cramps, tetany, carpopedal spasm, positive Chvostek and Trousseau signs, seizures, and a prolonged QT (torsades risk).

cards.prompt: What are the first two investigations for any thyroid nodule?
cards.answer: TSH and thyroid ultrasound. If TSH is suppressed → radionuclide scan (hot nodule = rarely cancer, no FNA); otherwise risk-stratify by ultrasound for FNA.

cards.prompt: Which thyroid cancer is most common, and which is C-cell derived?
cards.answer: Papillary is most common and usually indolent. Medullary arises from parafollicular C cells, secretes calcitonin, and is associated with MEN2/RET.

cards.prompt: What is the lab and treatment signature of Paget disease?
cards.answer: Isolated elevated ALP with normal calcium and phosphate, mixed lytic/sclerotic x-ray changes; first-line treatment is a single dose of IV zoledronic acid.

cards.prompt: Osteoporosis by BMD is a T-score of:
At what age is BMD screening recommended for everyone?
First-line pharmacologic therapy for most osteoporosis is:
Denosumab is a monoclonal antibody against:
First-line treatment of acute symptomatic hypercalcemia:
Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia is distinguished from primary HPT by:
Hypocalcemia that won't correct despite calcium suggests low:
A thyroid nodule with a suppressed TSH should next have:
Biochemical surveillance after differentiated thyroid cancer uses:
First-line treatment of symptomatic Paget disease is:
options: A single IV zoledronic acid dose

cards.prompt: Select recognised bisphosphonate adverse effects/cautions:
Select PTH-INDEPENDENT causes of hypercalcemia:
Select causes of hypocalcemia:
Select sonographic features suggesting thyroid malignancy:
Select clinical risk factors that support BMD screening:

cards.explain: T-score thresholds for postmenopausal women / men ≥50.

cards.text: Intake targets: calcium {{1000}}–{{1200}} mg/day and vitamin D {{600}}–{{800}} IU/day.
cards.explain: Diet first; supplement only the deficit.

cards.text: Primary hyperparathyroidism: calcium {{high}}, PTH high/inappropriately normal, phosphate {{low}}.
cards.explain: PTH lowers phosphate by increasing renal excretion.

cards.text: Paget disease shows an isolated high {{ALP}} with normal {{calcium}} and {{phosphate}}.
cards.explain: ALP reflects the high bone turnover.

cards.text: A highly suspicious thyroid nodule warrants FNA at ≥ {{1}} cm; moderately suspicious at ≥ {{1.5}} cm.

cards.prompt: Order acute hypercalcemia treatment:
items: Glucocorticoids / dialysis for selected refractory cases

cards.prompt: Order the thyroid-nodule workup:
items: If normal/high TSH → risk-stratify by ultrasound
FNA based on size + suspicious features

cards.prompt: Order osteoporosis management:
items: Reassess response; don't lapse denosumab without transition

cards.prompt: Match the osteoporosis drug to its mechanism:
Match the cause to its PTH pattern:
Match the thyroid cancer to a feature:
Match the drug to its key caution:
What is the role of calcitonin in acute hypercalcemia?
cards.answer: A rapid-onset but short-lived agent used as a bridge while slower, durable IV bisphosphonate therapy takes effect (tachyphylaxis limits prolonged use).

cards.prompt: When is teriparatide avoided?
cards.answer: Hypercalcemia/hyperparathyroidism, Paget disease, prior skeletal radiation, and skeletal malignancy (osteosarcoma concern); use caution in renal stones/insufficiency.

cards.prompt: What tumour marker follows medullary thyroid cancer, and what syndrome must be excluded?
cards.answer: Calcitonin (and CEA); exclude MEN2 via RET testing and screen for pheochromocytoma before any surgery.

cards.prompt: What is vitamin D's main effect on calcium and phosphate?
cards.answer: It increases intestinal absorption of both calcium and phosphate, raising serum levels of each (PTH, by contrast, raises calcium but lowers phosphate).

cards.prompt: Why do glucocorticoids cause osteoporosis?
cards.answer: They impair osteoblast bone formation and increase resorption (and reduce calcium absorption), making them a leading secondary cause — high-risk users need bone protection.

cards.prompt: How does cinacalcet lower calcium in hyperparathyroidism?
cards.answer: It is a calcimimetic that activates the parathyroid calcium-sensing receptor, suppressing PTH secretion — used in selected primary, secondary, and parathyroid-carcinoma hypercalcemia.

cards.prompt: Which clinical features of a thyroid nodule raise concern for malignancy?
cards.answer: Rapid growth, a hard fixed mass, hoarseness/dysphonia, dysphagia, prior neck radiation, family history of thyroid cancer/MEN2, and cervical lymphadenopathy.

guides.title: Week 28 · 1 Bone Physiology & Remodeling
guides.html:
Foundations

Bone Physiology & Remodeling

Week 28 · Endocrine IV — Bone & Calcium
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Cortical vs trabecular bone, the cells and the RANKL/OPG axis that drug targets exploit, and what PTH and vitamin D do to calcium.

Two kinds of bone

TypeFeaturesWhere / relevance
Cortical (compact)Dense, ~80% of skeletal massShaft of long bones, distal radius, hip; preferentially lost in hyperparathyroidism
Trabecular (cancellous)Spongy, metabolically active, remodels fastVertebrae, hip — the classic osteoporotic fracture sites

The cells and the remodeling cycle

  • Osteoclasts resorb bone — inhibited by bisphosphonates and denosumab.
  • Osteoblasts form bone — stimulated by intermittent PTH (teriparatide).
  • Osteocytes sense load and regulate remodeling — produce sclerostin (target of romosozumab).
  • Cycle: activation → resorption → reversal → formation → mineralization → rest.
RANKL / OPG — the switch
RANKL (from osteoblasts) drives osteoclast formation/activity; OPG is a decoy receptor that mops up RANKL and brakes resorption. Denosumab is a monoclonal anti-RANKL antibody — a pharmacologic OPG.

Calcium hormones

HormoneNet effect
PTH↑ serum calcium, ↓ phosphate; ↑ bone resorption, ↑ renal Ca reabsorption + phosphate excretion, ↑ activation of vitamin D
Vitamin D (calcitriol)↑ intestinal Ca & phosphate absorption → ↑ serum Ca and phosphate
Antiresorptives (bisphosphonates, denosumab) hit osteoclasts; anabolics (teriparatide, romosozumab) push osteoblasts/sclerostin. PTH raises calcium and lowers phosphate — vitamin D raises both.

guides.title: Week 28 · 2 Osteoporosis: Diagnosis & Screening
guides.html:
DXA & risk

Osteoporosis: Diagnosis & Screening

Week 28 · Endocrine IV — Bone & Calcium
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The fragility-fracture definition, the T-score thresholds, who needs a BMD, and the secondary-cause workup you must not skip.

Definitions

Osteoporosis = low bone mass + microarchitectural deterioration → fragility. A fragility fracture is one from standing height or less. A hip or vertebral fragility fracture is osteoporosis clinically, even if the BMD isn't in the osteoporotic range.

DXA: T-score vs Z-score

CategoryT-score
Normal≥ −1.0
Osteopenia−1.0 to −2.4
Osteoporosis≤ −2.5
  • T-score = vs young-adult reference (use in postmenopausal women and men ≥50).
  • Z-score = vs age/sex-matched peers; a low Z-score → hunt for a secondary cause.

Who gets a BMD?

AgeIndication
≥ 70Everyone
65–69≥1 clinical risk factor
50–64Prior fragility fracture, or ≥2 risk factors (e.g. parental hip fracture, glucocorticoids, smoking, alcohol, low BMI)
Always screen for secondary causes
Baseline labs: calcium + albumin, creatinine/eGFR, ALP, CBC, TSH, 25-OH vitamin D, and SPEP (especially with a vertebral fracture — multiple myeloma can present this way). A low Z-score or unexpected fracture demands this workup.
A low-trauma vertebral or hip fracture is osteoporosis regardless of T-score. Parental hip fracture is a key risk factor that justifies BMD at 50–64. Vertebral fracture → think myeloma (SPEP).

guides.title: Week 28 · 3 Osteoporosis: Management & Drugs
guides.html:
Treatment

Osteoporosis: Management & Drugs

Week 28 · Endocrine IV — Bone & Calcium
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Who to treat, the first-line bisphosphonate and how to counsel it, and the denosumab-rebound trap plus the anabolic agents.

Non-pharmacologic (everyone)

Dietary calcium first (supplement only the deficit), vitamin D, weight-bearing + resistance exercise, balance/fall-prevention, smoking cessation, alcohol reduction, home-safety and medication review. Targets: Ca 1000–1200 mg/day, vitamin D 600–800 IU/day, protein ~0.8 g/kg/day.

When to start a drug

  • T-score ≤ −2.5, OR a hip/vertebral fragility fracture, OR ≥2 fragility fractures, OR high 10-year risk (FRAX/CAROC), OR high-risk chronic glucocorticoids.

The drugs

DrugMechanismWatch for
Bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, zoledronic acid)Bind bone → osteoclast apoptosis — first-lineGERD/esophagitis (oral), MSK pain, rare atypical femur fracture & ONJ, hypocalcemia; caution in renal impairment
Denosumab (SC q6 months)Anti-RANKL antibodyHypocalcemia, infection; rebound vertebral fractures if stopped/delayed
Teriparatide (daily SC)Intermittent PTH analog — anabolicHypercalcemia, first-dose hypotension; avoid in hypercalcemia, Paget, prior skeletal radiation
RomosozumabSclerostin inhibitor (anabolic + antiresorptive)Cardiovascular risk — avoid with recent MI/stroke
RaloxifeneSERM — reduces vertebral fracturesVTE risk, hot flashes
Two traps
Alendronate counseling: take in the morning on an empty stomach with a full glass of water and stay upright 30–60 min (prevents esophagitis, aids absorption). Denosumab is not retained in bone like bisphosphonates — stopping or delaying it causes rebound bone loss and vertebral fractures; never just stop, transition to a bisphosphonate.
Oral bisphosphonate is first-line for most. Denosumab must never lapse without a transition. Anabolics (teriparatide, romosozumab) are for very high-risk or antiresorptive failure.

guides.title: Week 28 · 4 Calcium Homeostasis & Hypercalcemia
guides.html:
High calcium

Calcium Homeostasis & Hypercalcemia

Week 28 · Endocrine IV — Bone & Calcium
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Why you correct for albumin, the PTH-splits-the-cause rule, and how to bring an acutely high calcium down.

Calcium fractions

Total calcium = albumin-bound + anion-bound + free ionized (the only active fraction). Low albumin falsely lowers total calcium — measure ionized calcium or correct (add ~0.2 mmol/L per 10 g/L albumin below normal).

Presentation ("stones, bones, groans, moans")

Renal (polyuria, stones, nephrocalcinosis), bone pain, GI (constipation, nausea), neuropsychiatric (fatigue, confusion, depression), cardiac (short QT).

First check the PTH

PTH-dependent (high/inappropriately normal PTH)PTH-independent (suppressed PTH)
Primary & tertiary hyperparathyroidism; familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH); lithium, thiazidesMalignancy (PTHrP, osteolysis, calcitriol-producing lymphoma); vitamin D intoxication; granulomatous disease; hyperthyroidism, immobilization
  • Primary hyperparathyroidism: high Ca, high/inappropriately normal PTH, low phosphate, often high urine Ca.
  • FHH vs primary HPT: use a 24-h urine calcium / Ca-creatinine clearance ratio (low in FHH). Parathyroid imaging is for surgical planning, not diagnosis.
Acute hypercalcemia — in order
1) IV isotonic saline (volume + calciuresis) first-line. 2) Calcitonin for rapid but short-lived bridging. 3) IV bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid/pamidronate) for durable control (slower onset). 4) Glucocorticoids for granulomatous/calcitriol-mediated or some hematologic causes. 5) Dialysis if severe/refractory or renal failure. Then treat the cause.
PTH splits the differential: high PTH = parathyroid (or lithium/thiazide/FHH); low PTH = malignancy or vitamin D. Acute treatment starts with saline, then calcitonin bridges to a bisphosphonate.

guides.title: Week 28 · 5 Hypocalcemia
guides.html:
Low calcium

Hypocalcemia

Week 28 · Endocrine IV — Bone & Calcium
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The neuromuscular signs, the cause list (including the post-parathyroidectomy hungry bone trap), and how to replace safely.

Presentation

Increased neuromuscular excitability: perioral numbness, paresthesias, cramps, tetany, carpopedal spasm, positive Chvostek (facial tap) and Trousseau (cuff-induced spasm) signs, seizures; cardiac prolonged QT with risk of torsades.

Causes

CategoryExamples
PTH deficiencyPost-thyroidectomy/parathyroidectomy, autoimmune hypoparathyroidism
Vitamin D deficiencyLow intake/sun, malabsorption
CKDLow calcitriol + phosphate retention
SequestrationPancreatitis, rhabdomyolysis, hungry bone syndrome
DrugsBisphosphonates, denosumab, cinacalcet
Hungry bone syndrome
After parathyroidectomy for severe hyperparathyroidism, removing chronic PTH excess lets previously high-turnover bone rapidly take up calcium and phosphate → profound, symptomatic hypocalcemia within days. Recognize the tetany/long-QT picture post-op.

Management

  • Acute/symptomatic (tetany, seizures, long QT): IV calcium gluconate with cardiac monitoring.
  • Always check and replace magnesium — low Mg causes refractory hypocalcemia.
  • Chronic: oral calcium + vitamin D3 if deficient; calcitriol if hypoparathyroidism or CKD (impaired activation).
Post-parathyroidectomy tetany = hungry bone → IV calcium, not a bisphosphonate. Don't forget magnesium — hypocalcemia won't correct until Mg is replaced.

guides.title: Week 28 · 6 Thyroid Nodules
guides.html:
Nodule workup

Thyroid Nodules

Week 28 · Endocrine IV — Bone & Calcium
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The TSH-then-ultrasound algorithm, the sonographic red flags, and the size thresholds that trigger an FNA.

Initial assessment

History red flags: dysphagia, hoarseness, rapid growth, prior neck radiation, family history of thyroid cancer / MEN2. Exam: hard fixed mass, cervical lymphadenopathy.

The algorithm: TSH + ultrasound first
Every nodule gets a TSH and a thyroid ultrasound. If TSH is suppressed → radionuclide scan: a hot (functioning) nodule is rarely malignant — treat the thyrotoxicosis, no FNA. If TSH is normal/high → risk-stratify by ultrasound and decide on FNA by size + features. FNA is the only way to rule out cancer.

Sonographic risk factors for malignancy

  • Microcalcifications, irregular margins, taller-than-wide shape, hypoechogenicity, central vascularity, suspicious cervical nodes, larger size.

FNA size thresholds (by suspicion)

Sonographic suspicionFNA at size
Highly suspicious≥ 1 cm
Moderately suspicious≥ 1.5 cm
Mildly suspicious≥ 2.5 cm
Very low suspicion / spongiform / pure cystUsually observe (consider ≥2 cm or no FNA)

Interpreting the FNA (Bethesda)

CytologyTypical action
Non-diagnosticRepeat FNA under ultrasound
BenignReassure; surveillance ultrasound
Indeterminate (AUS/FLUS, follicular neoplasm)Molecular testing or diagnostic lobectomy — cytology can't separate follicular adenoma from carcinoma
Suspicious / malignantSurgery

Follicular lesions are the classic indeterminate trap: malignancy is defined by capsular/vascular invasion, which an aspirate cannot show — so a follicular neoplasm needs excision (lobectomy) to be classified.

Suppressed TSH → scan before you biopsy (hot nodule = don't FNA). Normal TSH + a ≥1 cm highly suspicious nodule → FNA. Imaging never replaces cytology, and a follicular cytology result needs surgery to distinguish adenoma from carcinoma.

guides.title: Week 28 · 7 Thyroid Cancer
guides.html:
Malignancy

Thyroid Cancer

Week 28 · Endocrine IV — Bone & Calcium
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The histologic types and what makes each behave, the management backbone, and how recurrence is monitored.

Most thyroid cancer is a painless nodule

Differentiated thyroid cancers usually present as an asymptomatic, firm, non-tender nodule with normal thyroid function.

TypeOriginBehaviour
PapillaryFollicular cellsMost common; usually indolent; lymphatic spread
FollicularFollicular cellsLess common; hematogenous spread
Poorly differentiatedFollicular cellsMore aggressive
AnaplasticFollicular cellsRare, very aggressive, elderly
MedullaryParafollicular C cellsCalcitonin marker; think MEN2 / RET

Management

  • Surgery is primary (lobectomy or total thyroidectomy by risk).
  • Radioactive iodine, TSH-suppressive levothyroxine, and external-beam radiation are used selectively for differentiated cancers.
Surveillance after differentiated thyroid cancer
Biochemical recurrence: serum thyroglobulin + anti-thyroglobulin antibodies (the antibody can invalidate the thyroglobulin if present). Structural recurrence: neck/thyroid ultrasound. (Calcitonin is the marker for medullary cancer, not differentiated.)
Papillary = commonest, indolent. Medullary = C cells, calcitonin, MEN2 — check for a pheo and RET. Differentiated-cancer follow-up = thyroglobulin (+ anti-Tg) and neck ultrasound.

guides.title: Week 28 · 8 Paget Disease of Bone
guides.html:
Remodeling gone wrong

Paget Disease of Bone

Week 28 · Endocrine IV — Bone & Calcium
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Focal disorganized bone turnover — how it presents, the isolated-ALP lab clue, and the single-dose treatment.

What it is

A focal disorder of excessive, disorganized bone remodeling (both resorption and formation) producing weak woven bone with increased vascularity. Common sites: skull, spine, pelvis, and long bones of the leg.

Presentation

  • Often asymptomatic (found on incidental ALP or x-ray).
  • Deep aching bone pain, warmth/erythema over the bone, bowing deformity, secondary osteoarthritis, nerve impingement (e.g. hearing loss with skull involvement), fracture.
The lab signature
Isolated elevated ALP with normal calcium and phosphate (unless immobilized or another process). High ALP reflects the bone turnover. X-ray: mixed lytic/sclerotic lesions, cortical thickening, pseudofractures. Bone scan maps the extent.

Complications

  • Deafness (skull involvement compressing CN VIII), other nerve impingement, and headache.
  • Secondary osteoarthritis of adjacent joints; bowing → gait/mechanical problems; fracture through weak woven bone.
  • High-output cardiac failure from the hypervascular bone (extensive disease).
  • Osteosarcoma — rare but feared malignant transformation; new severe pain or a soft-tissue mass warrants imaging.

Treatment & monitoring

  • Treat if symptomatic, at high risk of complications, or before surgery on Pagetic bone (reduces intra-op bleeding).
  • First-line: a single dose of IV zoledronic acid (oral alendronate for ~6 months is an alternative); ensure vitamin D/calcium replete first to avoid hypocalcemia.
  • Track response with ALP — a falling ALP reflects reduced bone turnover; a later rise can signal relapse.
Bowing, warmth, bone pain + isolated high ALP with normal calcium/phosphate = Paget. One IV zoledronic acid dose is the treatment, and ALP tracks the response. New severe pain or a mass → exclude osteosarcoma. (Contrast: osteomalacia has low/normal calcium and phosphate with high ALP and Looser zones.)

guides.title: Week 28 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

Endocrine IV — Bone & Calcium

Week 28 · Endocrine IV — Bone & Calcium
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Symptomatic hypocalcemia (tetany, seizures, long QT) → IV calcium gluconate + cardiac monitoring; replace magnesium
Hungry bone syndrome post-parathyroidectomy → profound hypocalcemia — IV calcium, NOT a bisphosphonate
Severe symptomatic hypercalcemia → IV isotonic saline first, calcitonin bridge, then IV bisphosphonate
Vertebral fragility fracture → clinically osteoporosis — treat; consider SPEP (myeloma)
Denosumab lapse → rebound vertebral fractures — never just stop; transition to a bisphosphonate
Medullary thyroid cancer → screen MEN2/RET + exclude pheochromocytoma before surgery

Bone physiology Physiology

Cortical bone dense; lost in hyperparathyroidism
Trabecular bone spine/hip; osteoporotic fractures
Osteoclast inhibitors bisphosphonates, denosumab
Osteoblast stimulators teriparatide (intermittent PTH)
RANKL vs OPG RANKL drives resorption; denosumab = anti-RANKL
PTH vs vitamin D PTH ↑Ca ↓PO4; vit D ↑both

Osteoporosis Dx Osteoporosis

T-score osteoporosis ≤ −2.5
Fragility fracture hip/vertebral = osteoporosis regardless of T
BMD: everyone age ≥70
Low Z-score look for secondary cause
Workup Ca/alb, Cr, ALP, CBC, TSH, 25-OH-D, SPEP
Parental hip fracture key risk factor (age 50–64)

Osteoporosis drugs Drugs

First-line oral bisphosphonate
Alendronate AM, empty stomach, upright 30–60 min
Denosumab anti-RANKL; never lapse (rebound)
Teriparatide anabolic; avoid Paget/radiation
Romosozumab avoid recent MI/stroke
Raloxifene SERM; VTE risk

Hypercalcemia High Ca

Correct for albumin ionized Ca is what matters
Check PTH first high = parathyroid; low = malignancy/vit D
Primary HPT high Ca, high PTH, low PO4
FHH low urine calcium; family history
Acute saline → calcitonin → IV bisphosphonate
Thiazide/lithium PTH-dependent-pattern drug causes

Hypocalcemia Low Ca

Signs Chvostek, Trousseau, tetany, long QT
Hungry bone post-parathyroidectomy
Refractory check and replace magnesium
Acute IV calcium gluconate + monitoring
Chronic oral calcium; calcitriol if hypoPTH/CKD

Thyroid nodule/cancer Thyroid

First tests TSH + ultrasound
Suppressed TSH radionuclide scan (hot = no FNA)
FNA only way to rule out cancer; ≥1 cm if high-suspicion
Papillary commonest, indolent
Medullary C cells, calcitonin, MEN2/RET
DTC surveillance thyroglobulin + anti-Tg, neck US

Paget Paget

Picture bone pain, warmth, bowing
Labs isolated high ALP; normal Ca/PO4
X-ray mixed lytic/sclerotic, cortical thickening
Treat if symptomatic / pre-op
First-line single IV zoledronic acid
Week 28 · Endocrine IV — Bone & Calcium · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.mechanism: Bisphosphonate — binds bone, induces osteoclast apoptosis
facets.adverse: Esophagitis, MSK pain, rare atypical femur fracture/ONJ, hypocalcemia
facets.dosing: AM, empty stomach, full water, upright 30–60 min

facets.use: Osteoporosis (annual IV); Paget (single dose); acute hypercalcemia
facets.adverse: Acute-phase flu-like reaction, hypocalcemia, renal; rare ONJ/AFF

facets.monitoring: Never lapse without transition (rebound vertebral fractures)

facets.contra: Paget, prior skeletal radiation, hypercalcemia, skeletal malignancy

facets.mechanism: Active vitamin D (1,25-OH) — boosts intestinal calcium absorption

facets.use: Hyperparathyroidism (primary unfit for surgery, secondary, parathyroid carcinoma)
facets.mechanism: Calcimimetic — activates the parathyroid calcium-sensing receptor → ↓PTH

facets.adverse: Can cause mild hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, hyponatremia
